title: Mechanical detection of sub-band mobilities of two-dimensional electron gas on reduced SrTiO$_3$(001) surface
authors: Akash Gupta, Marcin Kisiel, Remy Pawlak, Ernst Meyer
abstract: The two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) in reduced strontium titanate offers a versatile platform for oxide electronics, yet its dissipation mechanisms under field driven charge fluctuations remain poorly understood. Here, we combine low-temperature atomic force microscopy with scanning tunnelling spectroscopy to probe the force and dissipation responses of a mechanical oscillator interacting with the STO 2DEG. The observation of Rydberg like image potential states by tunnelling experiments confirm the 2DEG formation, while dissipation spectroscopy reveals bias-dependent peaks linked to local electrostatic gating and charge redistribution within the 2DEG energy sub-bands. These features are quantitatively explained by variations in quantum capacitance as carrier density is tuned by electric fields. Under magnetic fields, dissipation peaks obey the Kohler's rule, allowing extraction of carrier mobilities in each sub-band. Our results establish a non-invasive AFM - based methodology for quantifying energy losses in quantum oxides, providing new insights into charge dynamics relevant for spintronic applications.
summary: Researchers used AFM and STM to probe the energy loss mechanisms in the 2DEG of reduced strontium titanate. By analyzing mechanical dissipation and magnetoresistance, they were able to quantify the mobility of individual electronic sub-bands. This provides a new non-invasive way to study charge dynamics in quantum oxides.
main_problem: Understanding the dissipation mechanisms and energy losses under field-driven charge fluctuations in the two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) of reduced strontium titanate.
main_result: The study successfully extracted sub-band mobilities (2500-4800 cm^2/Vs) using Kohler's rule and identified that dissipation peaks are driven by variations in quantum capacitance and charge redistribution.
method: Combined low-temperature non-contact Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) and Scanning Tunneling Spectroscopy (STS) with application of external magnetic fields.
model_or_system: A two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) on a reduced SrTiO3(001) surface featuring three sub-bands (one heavy, two light) and oxygen vacancies acting as localized charge sources.
key_observables: Mechanical dissipation (Gamma), frequency shift (Delta f), differential conductance (dI/dV), and voltage-dependent force/capacitance peaks.
important_parameters: Carrier mobility (mu), effective mass (m*), magnetic field strength (B), and quantum capacitance (CQ).
main_assumptions: Single relaxation time approximation and the weak-magnetic field limit for applying Kohler's rule.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the STM overview of the surface, a schematic of electron exchange between vacancies and the 2DEG, and dI/dV point-spectra.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the 2DEG system, describes the combined AFM/STM experimental setup, presents the identification of sub-bands via spectroscopy, analyzes the dissipation mechanism through a capacitance model, and concludes with magnetic field-dependent mobility extraction.
why_interesting: The paper explores many-body dynamics, electron-phonon coupling, and spin-dependent scattering in a correlated oxide system, which is relevant to understanding dissipation in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Electronic properties of reduced STO surface. a, Overview STM image of the reduced STO surface after annealing at T = 1050◦C, (It = 1 pA, Vs = 1V) with inset showing a close-up STM image of the STO surface with atomic resolution. b, Electron exchange between vacancies and surface 2DEG caused by the potential of an oscillating AFM tip. c, dI/dV point-spectra showing the electronic band gap Eg. The inset shows ”Tanaka” peak (TP) characteristic of the oxygen-deficient STO surface, whereas OA and OB are vacancy states. d, Single FERT spectra of the reduced STO surface showing the series of nth IPS (It = 1 pA, Amod = 50 mV, fmod = 611 Hz). IS, CBM and V L refer to an interface state, the...

Fig. 2 Force and dissipation spectroscopy of the 2DEG. a, Principle of the experi- ment : the AFM tip is capacitively coupled to the 2DEG of reduced STO surface. Ctip and Csub refer to the capacitance of the tip and substrate, respectively. CQ is the 2DEG quan- tum capacitance. b, Quantum capacitance CQ as function of energy. CQ(E) is modeled by a Heaviside function, whose step positions and heights derive from the energy of the 2DEG sub-bands E1,2,3 and their effective masses m∗ 1,2,3. c, Tip-sample force versus voltage F(V )

Fig. 3 Evolution of dissipation peaks as a function of tip-sample distance Z. a, Γ(V, Z) dissipation map between the reduced STO surface and the AFM tip. The VCPD value has been corrected for all dataset. Dark lines denoted Γ± 1,2,3 are the dissipation peaks shown in Fig. 2. The gray area for large voltages and/or close distances is attributed to tip- induced switching of the Vo states. b, Linear regime of the Γ(V, Z) map shown in a used to extract the slope (tip-sample electric field strength) for each dissipation peak. c, extracted electric fields for dissipation peak marked by n separate two different charging mechanisms: 2DEG-induced charging peaks (white region) and -Vo vacancy induced...

Fig. 4 Pendulum-AFM energy dissipation vs. magnetic field. a, dissipation inten- sity map as a function of ∆f and external magnetic field B. For specific dissipation peaks Γ1, Γ2 and Γ3, ∆f(B) follows parabolic dependence (orange guides) consistent with standard Kohler’s rule, which enables extraction electron mobilitiy µ for each sub-band. A discontinu- ity is visible for heavy Γ1 sub-band, suggesting possible magnetic polarization (see text). b, Series of ∆f(Z) spectra (black) and corresponding Γ(Z) (orange) acquired for different B- field.
